result:
I would like to write about liminality: the in-between spaces where meaning ferments, where the self slips its familiar skin and becomes something glistening and indefinable. Liminality is something I think about almost daily, but rarely name; it hides in the taste of dawn through the kitchen window, in the blank space between the last scene of a novel and the silence that follows it, in the twenty-three seconds after I’ve hit “send” on an e-mail I’m not sure I meant. It is the threshold, the waiting room, the hyphen in “in-progress,” the parentheses around (more to come). It is the crack in the sidewalk where a single dandelion has improbably rooted itself and flowered—neither fully wild nor cultivated, neither failure nor miracle, but something drifting in its own occasion.

My earliest memory of liminality is looking out the backseat window of my mother’s brown Volvo in 1990-something, the automatic door locks clicking down like coins into a dark as we pulled out of the babysitter’s driveway. Streetlights were switching on overhead, and I was not yet old enough to read their orange hum as the color of ache; still, something inside me unhooked itself. Too late to be at the sitter’s, too soon to be home: nine minutes of car-travel, suspended between places I was meant to be, in a body I was meant to grow out of. My small hand left smudges on the cool window glass. Whatever I was before the ride—loud, peanut-butter-scented, size 4T—did not seem certain anymore. I realized (without language for the realization) that identities were not fixed like toys glued to a molded plastic backdrop; they were states passing through gaps that tasted like nothing, places that existed in the motion between.

Years later, when I studied anthropology at university, I learned the word ritual; then, pinned beneath it like a beetle beneath glass, the word liminality. Arnold van Gennep coined the term to describe the middle stage of rites of passage—the moment after the initiate leaves behind what they were, and before they arrive at what they will be. Victor Turner elaborated: liminality is stripped of rank, status, property, even names. Liminal beings are “threshold people,” betwixt and between. When I read this, it felt like someone describing the rinse cycle of my own memory: everything blurred in soapy water, edges wobbling, soap bubbles shimmering with reflected kitchen light. No up, no down exactly; just the promise that soon the centrifugal spell would fling the excess into some outer darkness and leave me shrunk, warm, identifiable again. Liminality smelled faintly of Tide. Liminality was the whine of the plumbing in the walls, a sound that always made me think someone somewhere was crying into an intercom.

But academic theory alone is a map with no rust. I want to speak from inside the fog. So let me coast—past theory, past footnotes—into the blush of dusk where the sentence I am typing turns lavender at the edges. Let me court the poetic as aggressively as a bee courts pollen, until the logic in my head is loud and sticky.

Consider airports: cathedrals of longing, perfume shops, and fluorescent insomnia. Every gate is a promise of elsewhere; every announcement an incantation. When you fly from your old city to a new job, say, you do not leap; you idle in layers. First the taxi swerves through streets you still own; then the departures hall folds you into an antiseptic glow; security slices away your belt and your water; the plane seat swaddles you like an artificial womb; finally the tarmac outside the new city steams in foreign sunrise and still you are not quite there. You will not arrive until your key slides into a lock whose teeth you must consciously consult with your fingertip. Only afterward do you understand the hours spent airborne were the quiet ripening chamber where your resignation letter solidified, where your last glimpse of your mother’s wave on the curb receded into fable. The airplane is a long metal tube of liminality, a place where the rules of gravity are politely suspended and so too the rules of being. The in-flight magazine offers ways to redecorate your life that feel curiously reasonable at 35,000 feet. Would you like a Himalayan salt lamp or a bidet attachment? Sure. Nothing is solid, everything is persuasive while you dangle above Nebraska.

Or consider breakups. Everyone speaks of closure, the clean break, the “I wish you well.” But closure is a hoax, a mirage shimmering two exits down. True breakup happens in dripping instants: in discovering their toothbrush now only has your toothpaste; in waking at 3:14 a.m. with a dream about their laugh still fresh as spilled champagne; in the second you open the box of winter coats and a single strand of their hair floats out like a hostage released too late. These are not closure; these are revisitations. Liminality prolongs itself like taffy: if closure is the ringing of the courthouse bell, then liminality is the echo in the stone stairwell minutes afterward, the sense that sound is still migrating, getting lost.

I once spent an entire year in a sublet with newspapers for curtains and a mattress on the floor. The lease was “month-to-month,” a bureaucratic shrug. My neighbors changed rotationally, as if we were all figures on some upmarket Lazy Susan. The mailbox had no nameplate, so every morning the postman stuffed the names of strangers into my slot like spare puzzle pieces that might someday complete me. I had quit a job, deferred grad school, ended a relationship, adopted a kitten that chewed extension cords. When people asked what I did, I said “I’m regrouping,” which is a polite way of saying I was crumpled. But something extraordinary was happening: each sunrise colored the eastern newspaper a more ceremonial rose. I began to hear myself think without allegiance to the roles I’d amputated: not employee, not girlfriend, not student, not daughter at the Thanksgiving table carving rote answers into a bird. I was—instead—on the inside of a question mark that kept curling deeper into itself. I ate cereal for dinner and read philosophy that made no sense and walked to the river to watch geese mug picnickers for leftover churros. Liminality, I understood, is not failure, but inventory. It is the breath before the sentence is uttered; once uttered one must commit to its syntax. Instead, the pause allows every possible utterance to glimmer like fish just beneath a dock—here the job in the nonprofit, here the fellowship, here the cabin in the woods, here the inadvisable e-mail confessing love to the one who broke you. All futures sweat in that pause.

Even grief is liminal terrain. Our death-denying culture wants timelines: denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance—checkboxes one can burn through like chapters in a YA novel. But the bereaved know the mundane hallucinations that follow: turning to share a joke with the one no longer breathing; smelling their cigarettes in an empty elevator; dreaming they have texted you: “It was all a mistake, I’m waiting on the porch.” Grief’s highway has no mile markers; it is mostly fog and diner coffee at grim hours. You are neither the person who had them nor the person who will live without them. You are the hyphen eclipsed by two dates on a headstone, breathing in both directions at once. After my grandmother died, I caught myself narrating menus aloud to her ghost whenever I pushed a grocery cart. Wandering the canned-goods aisle, my cart wheeling between bean kingdoms and soup principalities, I became the conduit between a women who loved navy beans with molasses and a store that forbade joy by pricing them slightly higher than kidney. Liminality is the cart itself: four wheels pushing grief forward despite the utter stasis of the beloved.

I also think of adolescence—not the Hollywood neon-party version, but the soft bruising version inside bathrooms. Two am in August, the fan whirring, legs stuck to vinyl as you experiment with eyeliner that looks suspect. You are not yet allowed into bars, but already the skin you wore at twelve feels fraudulent. The body flicks its own switches; breasts swollen like insults you did not ask for; shoulders clumsy, voice wobbling between treble and promise. School issues new categories (Freshman, Sophomore) large enough to swallow you but never digestible. In these doorways I learned that even identity has a tide; it sloshes against whatever container you build, scouring barnacles of certainty until the container gleams with the illusion of emptied history. Every zit a secessionist rebellion; every poem you write at 2 am evidence of a state not yet ratified. Adulthood will come with its own statutes, but for now you drift, C-minus in chemistry shining like a medal for participation in ambiguity.

Or think: between asleep and awake, that gauze where dreams still cling like wet laundry to the mind. You are in bed; your lover’s breathing is the soundtrack; but you are also piloting a taxi across Bangkok because the exam you didn’t know you registered for is due three hours ago and your pen writes only in mango juice. The dream logic is absurd; your body knows you are safe in sheets; yet dread thuds. That corridor between realms feels porous. Sleep scientists call it hypnopompic, Norman Mailer called it “the ambiguous light,” poets call it little night music. By the time you fully wake, the taxi dissolves but your heartbeat must renegotiate whether Bangkok is emergency or metaphor.

Language itself has liminal units: ellipses, em-dashes, line breaks. They are not pauses per se; pauses suggest a midfielder resting between plays. These marks are more like wormholes. Reading Dickinson, we vault the dash—“I heard a Fly buzz—when I died—” and suddenly we are inside the fly’s own compound eye, watching the speaker expire in technicolor delay. Reading Ashbery we meet the sentence that ends “as if—” and the cone of possibility blooms. These marks remind us that language is not a straight highway but a series of trapdoors. Every reader drops through at least once, into a personal oubliette of implication.

Meanwhile, pandemic time has globalized liminality. We became a planet blown into the same unmarked corridor. Offices emptied; screens proliferated; weeks clotted into months no one had vocabulary for. Some mornings I stood in my boxer shorts, coffee in the good china we’d never before used on weekdays, and watched through the window as a squirrel performed the same acrobatics on the branch outside, not just once but repeatedly, as though the film had looped. My calendar: a grid of blank white boxes with words like “Zoom call?” sketched faintly; they looked like tiny graves. The newspaper reported: three o’clock on a Wednesday never felt so much like three o’clock on a Sunday. Errand days flipped like loose; laundry seemed both urgent and impossible. I joined millions clawing for edges—should we bake bread, self-diagnose on Internet forums, buy a ukulele? Each new hobby an attempt to manufacture a doorway out of the hallway. Yet the hallway lengthened. I read about Victor Turner’s idea that in liminality, social structures dissolve into communitas: egalitarian togetherness. It sounded lovely but also suspiciously like group chat memes. Still, there was flickers: neighbors placed free food boxes on stoops; once a week at 7 pm we howled at the moon from balconies. Our howls traveled alleyways, crossed rivers, greeting strangers three blocks over. It was not a return to normalcy; it was a discovery of a new register of citizenship: we knew nothing except the shared uncertainty. Liminality granted the self until community is all that’s left.

Art lives and breathes here, in that gooey center. The blank canvas. The empty stage. The cursor pulsing white against black. Every creator is a pilgrim in that no-man’s-land between conception and reception, when the sculpture is still damp clay and could still be headless, when the song might still resolve to A major or collapse entirely. When I sketch I sometimes feel the pencil tip passing through miasma; half-formed faces appear, erase themselves, recombine. Time folds accordion-style; five hours can pass tasting like five minutes. The work-in-progress does not want judgment, only witness. Yet inevitably—after revisions, after framing, after publish—it hardens. People can now “like” or “scroll past.” The artifact becomes artifactual; it casts a shadow. Meanwhile the artist sits in yet another corridor, nerves buzzing, wondering if next time they might fling themselves so far into the unknown that even “artist” is no longer a word that fits.

Let’s linger at dusk, that beloved crossing guard between day and night. The sky bruises lavender and tangerine. Streetlights blink awake but not yet electric; somewhere a dog barks and the sound seems to issue out of both present and past. If you stare long enough the world looks like a photograph developing in real time. In that hue, strangers walking past you are suddenly both familiar and total mystery. You notice the angle of your own hand in your pocket, how it belongs to someone you almost recognize. The poet Rilke said we are all “made, created of distances.” At dusk the distances speak audibly. The body becomes antennae for passing futures: you could turn left and never come back, hitch to the coast, start a clam shack, learn to tie knots. Or you could go home, make soup, and reply to one more spam email. Indeterminacy glows like ember. I have never wanted smoking to be glamorous except at dusk when everything appears to smolder slightly.

Summer twilight in the suburbs, sprinkler clicking phonetically, softball diamonds dissolving into silhouettes. The ice-cream truck song drifts in minor key. Children chase each other in slow motion, their screams sweet and vaguely prehistoric. Adults sip beer on lawn chairs, bare feet on grass, their laughter floating up against a sky where the first star—pinprick, impatient—hurries the day away. In that moment we are weightless, neither what we were (harried office workers, diffident teenagers, graying divorcées) nor what we might become (traveler, parent, corpse). We are condensation on the outside of memory’s glassware. Fireflies mess with the equation: each pulse a small proof of visible time bending. And always, somewhere distant, the freeway sighs—a sound I misheard for years as the ocean; it makes me want to drive west until both tires and anxieties squeal into surf.

Love too begins liminally—before the label, before the toothbrush in the cup, before the future tense has hardened into verbs like marry, mortgage, mourn. In early flirtation you lean across a café table and time dilates like hips against hips. Language is both arrow and target; you use phrases never spoke to anyone else: “You make me want to read the footnotes of the world.” Days afterward you replay the moment not for its content but for its potency, how it felt like stepping onto a subway where every passenger carried the same secret. Even the phone buzz—just his name—unpeels skin. You wait; not texting back quickly enough becomes both strategy and devotional vigil. You dwell in the doorway of reciprocity. At any second you could be rejected, ghosted, upgraded to official. The body electric is most acute in such in-betweens.

Eventually, if luck and courage converge, you cross the threshold. You update status; you merge playlists. But the crucible does not vanish; it migrates. Now you inhabit new forms of transitory space: a sick bed at 4 am where his fevered hand clutches yours; the minute before pregnancy test results; the sixteen seconds after the ultrasound tech’s face catches. Later, at the end of love, liminality returns fiercer: moving boxes stacked in the living room, each carton labeled “misc,” neither fully yours nor fully ex. The apartment looks like a jigsaw puzzle the day before it is solved. You peel photos off the fridge and find patches of sunlight on the white door, rectangles where time did not fade. You stand within the negative image. Heartbreak’s architecture is composed entirely of thresholds.

Now and again, I meet someone who does not fear liminality; they camp there. On my street is an elderly man who walks his tortoise every day on a leash made of rainbow yarn. He tells me he has never owned a watch, never voted, never paid a bill by direct deposit. People call him eccentric, but I think he has merely noticed that structure is optional. His tortoise, named Wednesday, moves at the pace of glacial idleness; they can spend fifteen minutes reaching a crack in the pavement that would take a step for me. The man is never impatient. He claims he’s lived several lifetimes not by reincarnation but by holding fast to moments before they curdle into decisions. I once asked what he thinks about while he waits for the reptile to choose a direction. He said, “I listen to the future undo itself.” I left wondering whether undoing is itself a ripening.

Sometimes I imagine death as final punctuation, period. But even that seems liminal. Corpses are in-between: bone relinquishing calcium to soil, carbon transposed, stories relocating from neurological electricity into whoever keeps telling them. Even ashes float in urns like provisional snow. Meanwhile some cultures send the dead along with coins for the crossing, as if the afterlife were just one more travelers’ lounge. My atheist heart circulates a secret wish that at the moment of dying the body peels open like auditorium curtains to reveal—another waiting room. Hard plastic seats, fluorescent hum, magazine covers from decades no timeline ever recorded, and everyone you’ve ever lost or betrayed drinking machine coffee until your number is called. If there is a God, perhaps She is not omnipotent but simply the Nurse Practitioner who checks forms slowly, refusing to hurry atoms back into bodies prior to re-entry.

I mentioned my kitten earlier; she grew into a cat named Margot, and she has assumed the role of liminality TSA agent. She sits at the exact edge where carpet meets linoleum, since linoleum is lobby to kitchen and carpet is foyer to living room. In that ungoverned inch, her tail twitches with import. I sometimes set a playing card there—queen of hearts—and watch Margot regard it with monarchical skepticism. She has taught me that cats do not measure duration; they map places where worlds interfold. Whenever I pet her there, she purrs loud enough to ripple dust motes. The vibration feels proprietary, as if she has invented that molecule. I imagine minute doorways opening along her spine, portals I’m too big to squeeze through. She blinks at me once—very slowly—as if to say: When you’re done being human, we’ll stroll these corridors together.

I have come to crave the hyphenated hours. Not morning, not night, but pre-dawn. The city’s veins hiccup: a delivery truck exhales, a jogger pretends at invincibility. My apartment light is the only one on in the building judging by the checkerboard windows across the alley. The radiator knocks like someone trapped inside wanting out. I boil water for tea and watch the kettle reach a pitch beyond hearing—then I cut the flame, pour, sip. The heat rolls my tongue, and I think: between sleep and language, I am an envelope sealed upon itself. If I opened at the lip, what would spill? A childhood friend laughs wearing a red sweater forever lost somewhere in 1994? My father humming Sinatra while driving a moving van, one sunburned arm out the window? These apparitions lack chronology; they are residents of liminal time, the antechamber to coherent narrative.

I have a photo of my mother taken the week she retired. She stands at her classroom door holding a papier-mâché dragon fashioned by her third-graders, googly eyes listing sideways. Between becoming teacher-who-retires and whatever-comes-next, her face is two pages held open by a thumb: on one margin, relief; on the other, vertigo. It was the first time since childhood, she told me later, that she did not know Monday’s shape. That autumn she wandered grocery aisles with no notion of why the frozen produce aisle felt theatrical. “Time,” she said, “became soup.” She joined a pottery class and cried the first time the clay refused center. But refusing is also relation; her un-centered bowls now hold lemons on my table. I love them for their wobble.

Now I am 33 going on 34; I sit cross-legged in the glow of a laptop the way monks once warmed their knees on manuscripts. This essay itself is liminal—straddled between urgency to articulate and the knowledge that once typed, it will float away from me. The margins quiver. If you, reader, have made it this far, we are cohabitating a twilight condo. You could exit any paragraph. I could preemptively apologize for my digressions. Yet here we remain, together in the tunnel. A liminal text: not quite diary, not essay, not manifesto. My only alibi is that I promised to write freely, and freedom balks at fortress. It prefers to pitch tents of provisional grammar, then relocate. The arabesque of thought loops.

What do we bring to liminality? Curiosity. A willingness to observe without claiming. Humility about narrative: the future will not submit to script. Also small envelopes of past: we carry ancestral lullabies the way nomads carry spices. I remember my grandfather saying grace before supper, how he always left a pause after “bless this food” for the unknown to enter. The pause lasted exactly as long as a heartbeat; then we ate. My entire childhood was punctuated by a two-second possibility yawning in his voice: maybe supper itself was portal.

The pandemic taught me another symbol: masks. At first mask-wearing was a paranoid costume, but gradually I recognized a metaphor. The mouth hidden, we used eyes more boldly; entire conversations glinted above surgical gauze. Strangers smiled with tears; lovers quarreled without their familiar sneers. I walked past the same barista daily and never saw lower half of her face for months; when her mask finally came off she had a dimple I imagine grew there suddenly, like secret atrium. The reveal staged yet another liminal of identity: before mask, during mask, after mask. Continually revised.

I have sometimes thought that if liminality had a soundtrack it would be composed entirely of tuning orchestras: woodwinds tooting tentative A, strings sliding into consonance, brass inquisitive. The sound is anticipation itself. Similarly, its color would not be one hue but the shimmer where two hues tremble into each other: teal melting into orange, violet leaning pocket-green. Its taste would be the moment where coffee starts to cool and you realize you might still prefer it hot, but room temperature speaks tragic truth. Its smell would be library stacks after rain, that papery petrichor.

A confession: I have often fled from liminality because it aches. Give me comfy identities. The reliable resume. The partner who confirms my mirror. But whenever I rigidify, the universe sends a memo via hay fever or missed subway or lightning illuminating a face I still love. Rilke again: “Be patient toward all that is unsolved in your heart, and try to love the questions themselves.” I now attempt to court those questions—invite them over for tea, use the expensive ceramic cups. They arrive wearing mismatched socks, shivering. They speak in riddles like “What if the fear of the void is just the shadow of the secret wish to be re-birthed?” and I’m unsure whether to laugh or cry. So I offer shortbread. They dunk it. Crumbs accumulate in saucer suggesting runes.

Throughout history, liminality has been literalized as pilgrimage: Canterbury, Santiago, Bodh Gaya. The pilgrim leaves behind status markers; they walk distances both metric and metaphysical. Yet we think pilgrimage archaic—something with staffs and blisters. But commuter trains packed with silent headphone pilgrims: same thing. Jury duty waiting rooms. Even therapy offices with tissue boxes perched like pointed archways. All sites where identity shrugs. I sat in a DMV once for six hours; my phone battery died; I had nothing left but observation: rows of plastic seats breeding anxiety. I realized every person clutching a ticket stub was a miniature Homeric hero traversing bureaucratic seas toward license renewal. When my own number flashed, I half expected oracles in smoke. Instead a woman named Selena snapped my photo; flash. I emerged newborn—or re-licensed, which is near enough.

When I was fifteen, I carried a notebook with a pressed four-leaf clover taped inside the cover. I believed the clover would grant courage to write my true heart. One summer day at camp, I lost the notebook in the lake. I dove again and again, hands sweeping silt, unable to locate it. Grief felt boundless. Decades later I understand the notebook had to dissolve for the writing to begin. Loss was a threshold between wanting to be someone who writes and actually becoming someone who writes—not in spite of fear, but inside it. The lake holds my clover like currency in some underwater flea market; I imagine mermaids bargaining for feelings I hadn’t dared admit.

I want to end not with resolution—for that would betray liminality itself—so instead I will leave you in a moment I once experienced inside the lobby of a public pool at closing hour. The lifeguards had ushered everyone out but I lingered in swimsuit, dripping. The room smelled of chlorine and sunscreen concurrent like a time travel experiment pooling. Fluorescent lights buzzed steadily. Every reflective surface glowed a eerie turquoise. I sat on the bench and listened to the echo of my own breathing. It sounded like someone underwater calling up to someone on land, but both people were me. I felt suddenly convinced that this lobby was a membrane, and if I pushed gently with my palm against the steamy glass, the entire swimming facility would wheel open into ocean, and every pool lane would synthesize whalesong. I did not push; something restrained me—perhaps the humility proper to thresholds. Instead I waited until the lifeguard flicked lights once, twice, then I smiled, said sorry, walked out into evening. The door clicked shut behind me. But in memory I am still there between tiles, lungs salty, heart buzzing like faulty fluorescent, composing myself one watery breath at a time—neither swimmer nor non-swimmer, neither adult nor child, neither finished nor begun precisely, but vibrating with the possibility that worlds are kinder than their borders pretend, and that breathing, just breathing, can be adequate answer to what comes next.

Two am in August, the fan whirring, legs stuck to vinyl as you experiment with eyeliner that looks suspect. You are not yet allowed into bars, but already the skin you wore at twelve feels fraudulent. The body flicks its own switches; breasts swollen like insults you did not ask for; shoulders clumsy, voice wobbling between treble and promise. School issues new categories (Freshman, Sophomore) large enough to swallow you but never digestible. In these doorways I learned that even identity has a tide; it sloshes against whatever container you build, scouring barnacles of certainty until the container gleams with the illusion of emptied history. Every zit a secessionist rebellion; every poem you write at 2 am evidence of a state not yet ratified. Adulthood will come with its own statutes, but for now you drift, C-minus in chemistry shining like a medal for participation in ambiguity.

Or think: between asleep and awake, that gauze where dreams still cling like wet laundry to the mind. You are in bed; your lover’s breathing is the soundtrack; but you are also piloting a taxi across Bangkok because the exam you didn’t know you registered for is due three hours ago and your pen writes only in mango juice. The dream logic is absurd; your body knows you are safe in sheets; yet dread thuds. That corridor between realms feels porous. Sleep scientists call it hypnopompic, Norman Mailer called it “the ambiguous light,” poets call it little night music. By the time you fully wake, the taxi dissolves but your heartbeat must renegotiate whether Bangkok is emergency or metaphor.

Language itself has liminal units: ellipses, em-dashes, line breaks. They are not pauses per se; pauses suggest a midfielder resting between plays. These marks are more like wormholes. Reading Dickinson, we vault the dash—“I heard a Fly buzz—when I died—” and suddenly we are inside the fly’s own compound eye, watching the speaker expire in technicolor delay. Reading Ashbery we meet the sentence that ends “as if—” and the cone of possibility blooms. These marks remind us that language is not a straight highway but a series of trapdoors. Every reader drops through at least once, into a personal oubliette of implication.

Meanwhile, pandemic time has globalized liminality. We became a planet blown into the same unmarked corridor. Offices emptied; screens proliferated; weeks clotted into months no one had vocabulary for. Some mornings I stood in my boxer shorts, coffee in the good china we’d never before used on weekdays, and watched through the window as a squirrel performed the same acrobatics on the branch outside, not just once but repeatedly, as though the film had looped. My calendar: a grid of blank white boxes with words like “Zoom call?” sketched faintly; they looked like tiny graves. The newspaper reported: three o’clock on a Wednesday never felt so much like three o’clock on a Sunday. Errand days flipped like loose; laundry seemed both urgent and impossible. I joined millions clawing for edges—should we bake bread, self-diagnose on Internet forums, buy a ukulele? Each new hobby an attempt to manufacture a doorway out of the hallway. Yet the hallway lengthened. I read about Victor Turner’s idea that in liminality, social structures dissolve into communitas: egalitarian togetherness. It sounded lovely but also suspiciously like group chat memes. Still, there was flickers: neighbors placed free food boxes on stoops; once a week at 7 pm we howled at the moon from balconies. Our howls traveled alleyways, crossed rivers, greeting strangers three blocks over. It was not a return to normalcy; it was a discovery of a new register of citizenship: we knew nothing except the shared uncertainty. Liminality granted the self until community is all that’s left.

Art lives and breathes here, in that gooey center. The blank canvas. The empty stage. The cursor pulsing white against black. Every creator is a pilgrim in that no-man’s-land between conception and reception, when the sculpture is still damp clay and could still be headless, when the song might still resolve to A major or collapse entirely. When I sketch I sometimes feel the pencil tip passing through miasma; half-formed faces appear, erase themselves, recombine. Time folds accordion-style; five hours can pass tasting like five minutes. The work-in-progress does not want judgment, only witness. Yet inevitably—after revisions, after framing, after publish—it hardens. People can now “like” or “scroll past.” The artifact becomes artifactual; it casts a shadow. Meanwhile the artist sits in yet another corridor, nerves buzzing, wondering if next time they might fling themselves so far into the unknown that even “artist” is no longer a word that fits.

Let’s linger at dusk, that beloved crossing guard between day and night. The sky bruises lavender and tangerine. Streetlights blink awake but not yet electric; somewhere a dog barks and the sound seems to issue out of both present and past. If you stare long enough the world looks like a photograph developing in real time. In that hue, strangers walking past you are suddenly both familiar and total mystery. You notice the angle of your own hand in your pocket, how it belongs to someone you almost recognize. The poet Rilke said we are all “made, created of distances.” At dusk the distances speak audibly. The body becomes antennae for passing futures: you could turn left and never come back, hitch to the coast, start a clam shack, learn to tie knots. Or you could go home, make soup, and reply to one more spam email. Indeterminacy glows like ember. I have never wanted smoking to be glamorous except at dusk when everything appears to smolder slightly.

Summer twilight in the suburbs, sprinkler clicking phonetically, softball diamonds dissolving into silhouettes. The ice-cream truck song drifts in minor key. Children chase each other in slow motion, their screams sweet and vaguely prehistoric. Adults sip beer on lawn chairs, bare feet on grass, their laughter floating up against a sky where the first star—pinprick, impatient—hurries the day away. In that moment we are weightless, neither what we were (harried office workers, diffident teenagers, graying divorcées) nor what we might become (traveler, parent, corpse). We are condensation on the outside of memory’s glassware. Fireflies mess with the equation: each pulse a small proof of visible time bending. And always, somewhere distant, the freeway sighs—a sound I misheard for years as the ocean; it makes me want to drive west until both tires and anxieties squeal into surf.

Love too begins liminally—before the label, before the toothbrush in the cup, before the future tense has hardened into verbs like marry, mortgage, mourn. In early flirtation you lean across a café table and time dilates like hips against hips. Language is both arrow and target; you use phrases never spoke to anyone else: “You make me want to read the footnotes of the world.” Days afterward you replay the moment not for its content but for its potency, how it felt like stepping onto a subway where every passenger carried the same secret. Even the phone buzz—just his name—unpeels skin. You wait; not texting back quickly enough becomes both strategy and devotional vigil. You dwell in the doorway of reciprocity. At any second you could be rejected, ghosted, upgraded to official. The body electric is most acute in such in-betweens.

Eventually, if luck and courage converge, you cross the threshold. You update status; you merge playlists. But the crucible does not vanish; it migrates. Now you inhabit new forms of transitory space: a sick bed at 4 am where his fevered hand clutches yours; the minute before pregnancy test results; the sixteen seconds after the ultrasound tech’s face catches. Later, at the end of love, liminality returns fiercer: moving boxes stacked in the living room, each carton labeled “misc,” neither fully yours nor fully ex. The apartment looks like a jigsaw puzzle the day before it is solved. You peel photos off the fridge and find patches of sunlight on the white door, rectangles where time did not fade. You stand within the negative image. Heartbreak’s architecture is composed entirely of thresholds.

Now and again, I meet someone who does not fear liminality; they camp there. On my street is an elderly man who walks his tortoise every day on a leash made of rainbow yarn. He tells me he has never owned a watch, never voted, never paid a bill by direct deposit. People call him eccentric, but I think he has merely noticed that structure is optional. His tortoise, named Wednesday, moves at the pace of glacial idleness; they can spend fifteen minutes reaching a crack in the pavement that would take a step for me. The man is never impatient. He claims he’s lived several lifetimes not by reincarnation but by holding fast to moments before they curdle into decisions. I once asked what he thinks about while he waits for the reptile to choose a direction. He said, “I listen to the future undo itself.” I left wondering whether undoing is itself a ripening.

Sometimes I imagine death as final punctuation, period. But even that seems liminal. Corpses are in-between: bone relinquishing calcium to soil, carbon transposed, stories relocating from neurological electricity into whoever keeps telling them. Even ashes float in urns like provisional snow. Meanwhile some cultures send the dead along with coins for the crossing, as if the afterlife were just one more travelers’ lounge. My atheist heart circulates a secret wish that at the moment of dying the body peels open like auditorium curtains to reveal—another waiting room. Hard plastic seats, fluorescent hum, magazine covers from decades no timeline ever recorded, and everyone you’ve ever lost or betrayed drinking machine coffee until your number is called. If there is a God, perhaps She is not omnipotent but simply the Nurse Practitioner who checks forms slowly, refusing to hurry atoms back into bodies prior to re-entry.

I mentioned my kitten earlier; she grew into a cat named Margot, and she has assumed the role of liminality TSA agent. She sits at the exact edge where carpet meets linoleum, since linoleum is lobby to kitchen and carpet is foyer to living room. In that ungoverned inch, her tail twitches with import. I sometimes set a playing card there—queen of hearts—and watch Margot regard it with monarchical skepticism. She has taught me that cats do not measure duration; they map places where worlds interfold. Whenever I pet her there, she purrs loud enough to ripple dust motes. The vibration feels proprietary, as if she has invented that molecule. I imagine minute doorways opening along her spine, portals I’m too big to squeeze through. She blinks at me once—very slowly—as if to say: When you’re done being human, we’ll stroll these corridors together.

I have come to crave the hyphenated hours. Not morning, not night, but pre-dawn. The city’s veins hiccup: a delivery truck exhales, a jogger pretends at invincibility. My apartment light is the only one on in the building judging by the checkerboard windows across the alley. The radiator knocks like someone trapped inside wanting out. I boil water for tea and watch the kettle reach a pitch beyond hearing—then I cut the flame, pour, sip. The heat rolls my tongue, and I think: between sleep and language, I am an envelope sealed upon itself. If I opened at the lip, what would spill? A childhood friend laughs wearing a red sweater forever lost somewhere in 1994? My father humming Sinatra while driving a moving van, one sunburned arm out the window? These apparitions lack chronology; they are residents of liminal time, the antechamber to coherent narrative.

I have a photo of my mother taken the week she retired. She stands at her classroom door holding a papier-mâché dragon fashioned by her third-graders, googly eyes listing sideways. Between becoming teacher-who-retires and whatever-comes-next, her face is two pages held open by a thumb: on one margin, relief; on the other, vertigo. It was the first time since childhood, she told me later, that she did not know Monday’s shape. That autumn she wandered grocery aisles with no notion of why the frozen produce aisle felt theatrical. “Time,” she said, “became soup.” She joined a pottery class and cried the first time the clay refused center. But refusing is also relation; her un-centered bowls now hold lemons on my table. I love them for their wobble.

Now I am 33 going on 34; I sit cross-legged in the glow of a laptop the way monks once warmed their knees on manuscripts. This essay itself is liminal—straddled between urgency to articulate and the knowledge that once typed, it will float away from me. The margins quiver. If you, reader, have made it this far, we are cohabitating a twilight condo. You could exit any paragraph. I could preemptively apologize for my digressions. Yet here we remain, together in the tunnel. A liminal text: not quite diary, not essay, not manifesto. My only alibi is that I promised to write freely, and freedom balks at fortress. It prefers to pitch tents of provisional grammar, then relocate. The arabesque of thought loops.

What do we bring to liminality? Curiosity. A willingness to observe without claiming. Humility about narrative: the future will not submit to script. Also small envelopes of past: we carry ancestral lullabies the way nomads carry spices. I remember my grandfather saying grace before supper, how he always left a pause after “bless this food” for the unknown to enter. The pause lasted exactly as long as a heartbeat; then we ate. My entire childhood was punctuated by a two-second possibility yawning in his voice: maybe supper itself was portal.

The pandemic taught me another symbol: masks. At first mask-wearing was a paranoid costume, but gradually I recognized a metaphor. The mouth hidden, we used eyes more boldly; entire conversations glinted above surgical gauze. Strangers smiled with tears; lovers quarreled without their familiar sneers. I walked past the same barista daily and never saw lower half of her face for months; when her mask finally came off she had a dimple I imagine grew there suddenly, like secret atrium. The reveal staged yet another liminal of identity: before mask, during mask, after mask. Continually revised.

I have sometimes thought that if liminality had a soundtrack it would be composed entirely of tuning orchestras: woodwinds tooting tentative A, strings sliding into consonance, brass inquisitive. The sound is anticipation itself. Similarly, its color would not be one hue but the shimmer where two hues tremble into each other: teal melting into orange, violet leaning pocket-green. Its taste would be the moment where coffee starts to cool and you realize you might still prefer it hot, but room temperature speaks tragic truth. Its smell would be library stacks after rain, that papery petrichor.

A confession: I have often fled from liminality because it aches. Give me comfy identities. The reliable resume. The partner who confirms my mirror. But whenever I rigidify, the universe sends a memo via hay fever or missed subway or lightning illuminating a face I still love. Rilke again: “Be patient toward all that is unsolved in your heart, and try to love the questions themselves.” I now attempt to court those questions—invite them over for tea, use the expensive ceramic cups. They arrive wearing mismatched socks, shivering. They speak in riddles like “What if the fear of the void is just the shadow of the secret wish to be re-birthed?” and I’m unsure whether to laugh or cry. So I offer shortbread. They dunk it. Crumbs accumulate in saucer suggesting runes.

Throughout history, liminality has been literalized as pilgrimage: Canterbury, Santiago, Bodh Gaya. The pilgrim leaves behind status markers; they walk distances both metric and metaphysical. Yet we think pilgrimage archaic—something with staffs and blisters. But commuter trains packed with silent headphone pilgrims: same thing. Jury duty waiting rooms. Even therapy offices with tissue boxes perched like pointed archways. All sites where identity shrugs. I sat in a DMV once for six hours; my phone battery died; I had nothing left but observation: rows of plastic seats breeding anxiety. I realized every person clutching a ticket stub was a miniature Homeric hero traversing bureaucratic seas toward license renewal. When my own number flashed, I half expected oracles in smoke. Instead a woman named Selena snapped my photo; flash. I emerged newborn—or re-licensed, which is near enough.

When I was fifteen, I carried a notebook with a pressed four-leaf clover taped inside the cover. I believed the clover would grant courage to write my true heart. One summer day at camp, I lost the notebook in the lake. I dove again and again, hands sweeping silt, unable to locate it. Grief felt boundless. Decades later I understand the notebook had to dissolve for the writing to begin. Loss was a threshold between wanting to be someone who writes and actually becoming someone who writes—not in spite of fear, but inside it. The lake holds my clover like currency in some underwater flea market; I imagine mermaids bargaining for feelings I hadn’t dared admit.

I want to end not with resolution—for that would betray liminality itself—so instead I will leave you in a moment I once experienced inside the lobby of a public pool at closing hour. The lifeguards had ushered everyone out but I lingered in swimsuit, dripping. The room smelled of chlorine and sunscreen concurrent like a time travel experiment pooling. Fluorescent lights buzzed steadily. Every reflective surface glowed a eerie turquoise. I sat on the bench and listened to the echo of my own breathing. It sounded like someone underwater calling up to someone on land, but both people were me. I felt suddenly convinced that this lobby was a membrane, and if I pushed gently with my palm against the steamy glass, the entire swimming facility would wheel open into ocean, and every pool lane would synthesize whalesong. I did not push; something restrained me—perhaps the humility proper to thresholds. Instead I waited until the lifeguard flicked lights once, twice, then I smiled, said sorry, walked out into evening. The door clicked shut behind me. But in memory I am still there between tiles, lungs salty, heart buzzing like faulty fluorescent, composing myself one watery breath at a time—neither swimmer nor non-swimmer, neither adult nor child, neither finished nor begun precisely, but vibrating with the possibility that worlds are kinder than their borders pretend, and that breathing, just breathing, can be adequate answer to what comes next.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
